q: In 1678, the Dutch physicist Christiaan Huygens introduced the particle theory of light.
Huygens' principle states that every point on a wavefront may be considered as a source of secondary wavelets.
The new position of a wavefront is the envelope of the secondary wavelets.
Secondary wavelets spread out in all directions with a speed different from the original propagating wave.
A wavefront is defined as a surface associated with a propagating wave which is a locus of all points in the wave that have the same phase.
At a given moment of time, all particles of the medium on a particular wavefront are undergoing the same phase.
Wavefront is always parallel to the direction of propagation (or) rays.
A spherical wavefront from a source transforms into a plane wavefront when it is far away from the source.
The phase angle is the position of a point on a reference circle of a wavefront at an instant.
The angular position of a point of a periodic wave is known as the phase angle.
If phase difference are $0, 2\pi, 4\pi, 6\pi, \dots$, these two positions are completely out of phase.
If phase difference are $\pi, 3\pi, 5\pi, \dots$, these two positions are completely out of phase.
Path difference indicates the distance between two points measured along the direction of propagation of the wave through the medium.
If path difference is equal to $0, \lambda, 2\lambda, 3\lambda, \dots$, the points are said to be in phase.
If path difference are $\frac{\lambda}{2}, \frac{3\lambda}{2}, \frac{5\lambda}{2}, \dots$, it is said completely out of phase.
The relation between phase difference and path difference is expressed as $\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta x$.
In wave optics, ___ principle explained how the light propagates from a light source.
___ light means that it has a single wavelength.
What is the term for the locus of all points in a wave that have the same phase?
According to Huygens' principle, what is the speed of the secondary wavelets in a medium where the propagating wave has speed $v$?
If two positions of periodic waves are 'in phase' (same phase), what is a possible phase difference between them?
The wavefront is always ___ to the direction of propagation.
Which type of wavefront is produced by a source when viewed from a very large distance?
Wave optics (physical optics) deals with the study of interference and diffraction where the ray approximation of geometrical optics is not valid.
Huygens' principle states that the source of secondary wavelets can only be at the center of the source.
In Young's double slit experiment, alternate bright and dark lines seen on the screen are explained as interference phenomenon.
When a plane wave passes through a slit, a circular wavelet emerges from it according to Huygens' principle.
In the double slit experiment, each slit acts as a secondary source of circular waves.
Two waves are coherent if they are of same frequency, same amplitude, and are in phase.
The interference pattern consists of a series of bright and dark parallel lines called fringes.
Constructive interference occurs at the centre of the screen because waves travel the same distance and are in phase.
A crest of one wave arriving at the same time as a trough of the other wave results in constructive interference.
If the path difference is equal to an integral number of wavelengths $(0, \lambda, 2\lambda, \dots)$, the waves will interfere constructively.
Constructive interference results in dark fringes on the screen.
If the path difference is equal to a half-integral number of wavelengths $(\frac{\lambda}{2}, \frac{3\lambda}{2}, \frac{5\lambda}{2}, \dots)$, the waves will interfere destructively.
The position of fringes on the screen is given by the distance from the central bright fringe, denoted as $y$.
For a bright fringe to be formed, path difference must be equal to an integral number of wavelengths: $d \sin \theta = m \lambda$.
The zeroth order ($m = 0$) bright corresponds to the central bright line at $\theta = 0$.
The first-order brights ($m = 1$) can be seen on only one side of the central line.
The distance between two adjacent bright (or dark) fringes is called the fringe width.
The position of fringes and fringe width are independent of the wavelength of the light source.
The fringe width $\Delta y$ is given by the formula $\Delta y = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
Bright and dark fringes are formed alternatively where bright fringe has maximum intensity and intensity of dark fringe is zero.
In Young's experiment, two waves from each source will ___ and as a result, an interference pattern will be displayed on the screen.
The central bright fringe is formed because waves reaching the centre of the screen travel the ___ distance.
What is the condition for two waves to be coherent?
a: Same frequency, same amplitude and in phase

q: In the formula $y = m \frac{\lambda D}{d}$, what does $m$ represent?
What type of interference leads to a dark fringe?
What happens at the centre of the screen in a double-slit experiment?
The distance between the $m^{th}$ and $(m+1)^{th}$ order bright fringe is known as the ___.
Diffraction is defined as the bending of waves around the corners of an obstacle into the region of geometrical shadow.
The amount of bending depends on the wavelength of light relative to the size of the aperture.
If the aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light, the bending of light will be very noticeable.
Fresnel diffraction occurs when the source of light and the screen are at a finite distance from the obstacle causing diffraction.
In Fraunhofer diffraction, there is an infinite distance between the source and the screen from the obstacle.
The diffraction pattern of a single slit consists of a bright central portion called the central maximum.
Successive bright fringes in a diffraction pattern become more intense proceeding outward from the central maximum.
Secondary wavelets from the upper and lower halves of a slit reach the center point $P_0$ in the same phase to produce a central bright maximum.
The first minimum in single-slit diffraction is formed when the path difference between waves from the center and top of the slit is $\frac{\lambda}{2}$.
For the first minimum in single-slit diffraction, the condition is $a \sin \theta = \lambda$.
Generally for the $m^{th}$ minimum in single-slit diffraction, $a \sin \theta = m \lambda$ where $m = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
The width of the central maximum in a single-slit diffraction pattern is equal to the position of the first minimum.
A narrower slit causes a wider central maximum in the diffraction pattern.
The width of the central maximum depends on the width of the slit.
Diffraction is the ___ of waves around the corners of an obstacle.
In ___ diffraction, the source and screen are at an infinite distance from the obstacle.
The bright central portion of the diffraction pattern is called the ___.
In which type of diffraction are the source and screen at a finite distance?
What happens to the intensity of successive bright fringes as you move away from the central maximum in a diffraction pattern?
What is the condition for the $m^{th}$ minimum in single-slit diffraction?
A narrower slit in a single-slit experiment results in a:
a: Central maximum of the same width

q: A very simple demonstration of light diffraction can be conducted by placing the sharp edges of a razor blade in front of intense blue light.
The amount of bending in diffraction is almost unnoticeable if the aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light.
Diffraction fringes are created by the interference of diffracted light waves.
The width of the central maximum is given by $2 \times$ position of the ___.
A diffraction grating consists of a large number of parallel slits of same width separated by equal opaque spaces.
A diffraction grating can disperse light into its wavelength components.
Diffraction gratings are widely used as dispersive elements in spectrographic instruments.
There are two types of grating: transmission grating and refraction grating.
The number of lines (or slits) per unit length is called the grating constant.
The condition for maximum intensity in a diffraction grating is the same as that for a Young's double slit.
For maximum intensity to be formed at $P$, the path difference of two waves from adjacent slits must be equal to an integral number of wavelengths.
In a diffraction grating, $d \sin \theta = m \lambda$ where $d$ is the distance between adjacent slits.
If the number of slits per unit length is $N$, then $d$ is equal to $N$.
There is no dispersion of incident white light at the centre ($m = 0$) of a grating spectrum.
In the first order diffraction spectrum, longer wavelength (red light) diffracts at a smaller angle than shorter wavelength (violet light).
A compact disc is an example of reflection gratings.
The tracks of a compact disc act as a diffraction grating, producing a separation of colors of white light.
A diffraction grating can be manufactured by carving glass with a sharp tool.
The number of lines per unit length of a grating is called the ___.
In a grating, $d$ is the distance between ___ slits.
A ___ is a shiny surface having many fine grooves that acts as a reflection grating.
What is the primary purpose of a diffraction grating in optical analysis?
a: To focus light like a lens

q: In a grating with $N$ lines per unit length, how is the slit separation $d$ calculated?
Which color of light diffracts at a greater angle in the first order spectrum produced by a grating?
What happens at the center ($m=0$) when white light is incident on a diffraction grating?
a: It is dispersed into a spectrum

q: Transmission grating and reflection grating are the two types of grating mentioned in the text.
A diffraction grating is a crucial tool in the study of atomic and molecular compositions.
The condition for maximum intensity in a grating involves path difference equal to $m \lambda$.
The tracks of a compact disc act as a ___ grating.
